title: Gynecomastia with Markedly Elevated Estradiol in a 13-Year-Old Boy

relatedChapters.title: Gynecomastia in an Adolescent Male (Case Report)

items.title: How the case presents — read the estrogen–testosterone mismatch first
items.content: A 13-year-old boy (birth weight 4 kg) has bilateral painless gynecomastia and obesity (weight 72 kg, height 158 cm, BMI ~28.8). Testes are 4 mL bilaterally with a stretched penile length of 7 cm. Biochemistry shows a markedly elevated estradiol of 296 pg/mL with a low total testosterone of 0.11 ng/mL (~11 ng/dL), low gonadotropins (LH 0.61, FSH 1.92 mIU/mL), normal beta-hCG 0.4, normal prolactin (11) and TSH, with a mildly raised ALT (51) and LDL 117, plus a reported splenomegaly of 7 cm. The single most important pattern to recognise is the mismatch: an adult-female-range estradiol coexisting with a near-castrate testosterone and suppressed gonadotropins. That combination is not physiological puberty — it signals an autonomous estrogen source feeding back on the HPG axis, and it reframes the whole work-up.
keyPoints: E2 296 pg/mL (adult-female range) + T 0.11 ng/mL + low LH/FSH = a mismatch, not normal puberty
Normal beta-hCG argues against an hCG-secreting tumour driving the picture
Unexplained splenomegaly + raised ALT are extra red flags to chase

items.title: Why a high estradiol with low testosterone and low gonadotropins points to an autonomous estrogen source
items.content: In normal male puberty the testis makes testosterone, a fraction of which is aromatised to estradiol, so estradiol rises in step with (and downstream of) testosterone, and gonadotropins are appropriate. Here the order is inverted: estradiol is enormous while testosterone is castrate-low and LH/FSH are suppressed. Estrogen is a potent negative-feedback signal at the hypothalamus and pituitary, so a high estradiol coming from a source other than testosterone-driven aromatisation shuts down LH/FSH, which in turn keeps testicular testosterone low. This 'estrogen-first, gonadotropins-off' signature is the hallmark of an autonomous estrogen source — a tumour, an aromatase-excess state, or exogenous estrogen — rather than gonadotropin-driven puberty. It is exactly why the level cannot be dismissed as obesity or normal pubertal gynecomastia.
keyPoints: Normal puberty: testosterone leads, estradiol follows, gonadotropins appropriate
Here estradiol leads and suppresses LH/FSH → testosterone stays low
Pattern = autonomous estrogen source (tumour / aromatase excess / exogenous)

items.title: Why the first step is to repeat estradiol by LC-MS/MS, not to treat the number
items.content: Before chasing tumours or starting drugs, verify the analyte. Automated estradiol immunoassays are designed for adult-female ranges and are notoriously unreliable in males and children: they cross-react with other steroids and metabolites, are vulnerable to heterophile antibodies, and lose accuracy at the low and unusual concentrations seen in boys. An immunoassay estradiol of 296 pg/mL in a 13-year-old male may be partly or wholly artefactual. The gold standard is liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), which should be used to re-measure estradiol (ultrasensitive) and testosterone, ideally as part of a full steroid profile. Acting on an unverified immunoassay value risks both over-treatment (blocking estrogen that is not truly elevated) and a needless tumour hunt — so verification comes first.
keyPoints: Immunoassay estradiol is unreliable in boys/children (cross-reactivity, heterophile antibodies)
Re-measure estradiol + testosterone by LC-MS/MS before acting
Treating an unverified number risks over-treatment and a needless work-up

items.title: How a full LC-MS/MS steroid panel localises the source
items.content: If estradiol is genuinely high, the steroid panel does the localising work. Order, by LC-MS/MS, estradiol and estrone, total testosterone, androstenedione, DHEA-S and 17-hydroxyprogesterone. The pattern is informative: a feminising adrenal tumour typically raises adrenal androgens (DHEA-S, androstenedione) and estrone/estradiol together; an aromatase-excess state shows high estrogens generated from those adrenal/gonadal androgens with otherwise unremarkable precursors; an estrogen-secreting testicular tumour may show high estradiol with low gonadotropin-suppressed androgens. The panel therefore separates 'too much substrate being aromatised' (adrenal source / aromatase excess) from a directly estrogen-secreting gonadal tumour, and points the imaging. It also screens for a steroidogenic block (17-OHP) that could distort the picture.
keyPoints: Panel: estradiol, estrone, testosterone, androstenedione, DHEA-S, 17-OHP (all LC-MS/MS)
High DHEA-S/androstenedione + estrone ⇒ adrenal source; estrogens from androgens ⇒ aromatase excess
Separates excess-substrate aromatisation from a directly estrogen-secreting tumour

items.title: Why a scrotal ultrasound is mandatory even with normal-volume, non-palpable testes
items.content: Estrogen-secreting testicular tumours — Sertoli cell tumours (including the large-cell calcifying variant linked to Carney complex and Peutz–Jeghers) and Leydig cell tumours — can be small, sub-centimetre and non-palpable, yet drive florid hyperestrogenism and gynecomastia. Normal testicular volume and a normal examination do not exclude them. So a high-resolution scrotal ultrasound is mandatory in a boy with confirmed hyperestrogenism, looking for an occult intratesticular lesion and any associated microlithiasis/calcification. If a Sertoli tumour is suspected, inhibin B and AMH can support the diagnosis. Finding a tumour changes management decisively to surgery, so this scan is not optional reassurance — it is a targeted search for a curable cause.
keyPoints: Sertoli/Leydig cell tumours can be tiny, non-palpable, yet strongly estrogenic
Normal testicular volume/exam does NOT exclude them — image with high-res scrotal US
Large-cell calcifying Sertoli tumours link to Carney complex / Peutz–Jeghers

items.title: Why adrenal/abdominal imaging is required to exclude a feminising adrenocortical tumour
items.content: Feminising adrenocortical tumours, though rare in children, are an important and dangerous cause of hyperestrogenism: they secrete estrogens (often with adrenal androgens) autonomously and a proportion are malignant adrenocortical carcinomas. A boy with confirmed high estradiol and raised adrenal androgens on the steroid panel needs cross-sectional adrenal/abdominal imaging (CT or MRI) to find or exclude such a mass. This imaging does double duty in this patient: it also evaluates the reported splenomegaly and the mildly raised ALT, looking at the liver and spleen for the structural explanation those findings demand. So the abdominal scan answers two questions at once — is there a feminising adrenal tumour, and what is causing the hepatosplenic findings.
keyPoints: Feminising adrenocortical tumours secrete estrogen autonomously; some are carcinomas
Confirmed high E2 (esp. with high adrenal androgens) ⇒ CT/MRI adrenal/abdomen
The same scan assesses the unexplained splenomegaly and the liver

items.title: How aromatase excess syndrome produces this exact picture
items.content: Aromatase excess syndrome (AES) is a rare, usually autosomal-dominant disorder caused by gain-of-function rearrangements of CYP19A1, the aromatase gene, leading to over-conversion of adrenal and gonadal androgens into estrogens. It classically presents in boys with peripubertal/prepubertal gynecomastia, accelerated growth and advanced bone age, and — because the autonomously generated estrogen suppresses the HPG axis — low gonadotropins and low testosterone, exactly the mismatch seen here. A family history of gynecomastia in male relatives or early-fused short-statured relatives is a clue. AES is confirmed by genetic testing of CYP19A1 after the steroid panel shows estrogens elevated out of proportion to a directly estrogen-secreting tumour. Recognising AES matters because it has a specific, mechanism-directed treatment (an aromatase inhibitor) rather than surgery.
keyPoints: AES = gain-of-function CYP19A1 → over-aromatisation of androgens to estrogens
Boys: prepubertal gynecomastia, advanced bone age, suppressed LH/FSH and low testosterone
Confirm by CYP19A1 genetics; ask about familial gynecomastia/short stature

items.title: Why the normal beta-hCG matters and why exogenous estrogen must be excluded
items.content: Two parts of the differential are quickly addressed. First, hCG-secreting tumours (germ-cell tumours, hepatoblastoma, mediastinal germ-cell tumours) stimulate Leydig cells to make both testosterone and estradiol and classically cause gynecomastia — but they do so with a raised beta-hCG, so the normal beta-hCG of 0.4 here makes an hCG-driven mechanism unlikely. Second, exogenous estrogen exposure can reproduce florid hyperestrogenism and must be excluded by a meticulous history: transfer of topical estrogen/progesterone creams or gels from a family member through skin contact, estrogen-containing supplements or soy-heavy intake, repeated lavender or tea-tree-oil personal-care products (weakly estrogenic), and any aromatisable anabolic steroids. Exogenous exposure is the most readily reversible cause, so it is cheap and essential to chase before invasive work-up.
keyPoints: Normal beta-hCG argues against an hCG-secreting (germ-cell/hepatic) tumour
Take a detailed exogenous-estrogen history (creams/gels transfer, supplements, lavender/tea-tree, anabolics)
Exogenous exposure is the most reversible cause — exclude it early

items.title: Why the splenomegaly and raised ALT must not be filed as incidental
items.content: The reported 7 cm splenomegaly with a mildly elevated ALT is the clue the quick differentials tend to skip. Chronic liver disease itself causes hyperestrogenism and gynecomastia — through altered sex-hormone-binding globulin, reduced hepatic clearance of estrogens and increased peripheral aromatisation — and splenomegaly raises the possibility of portal hypertension or an infiltrative/storage disorder (for example a hepatosplenomegaly-causing storage disease). While chronic liver disease alone rarely produces an estradiol of 296, a hepatic process can both contribute to the hormonal picture and signal serious underlying pathology. So evaluate the liver and spleen properly: full liver function including synthetic markers (albumin, bilirubin, INR), an abdominal ultrasound with Doppler for liver texture and portal flow/spleen size, and targeted work-up for causes of hepatosplenomegaly. Do not attribute the splenomegaly to obesity.
keyPoints: Chronic liver disease causes hyperestrogenism/gynecomastia (SHBG, reduced clearance, aromatisation)
Splenomegaly ⇒ consider portal hypertension or an infiltrative/storage disorder
Work up: synthetic LFTs (albumin/INR/bilirubin) + abdominal US with Doppler

items.title: Why a bone-age X-ray is essential when estrogen is high
items.content: Estrogen, not testosterone, is the dominant driver of skeletal maturation and epiphyseal fusion in both sexes. Sustained high estradiol therefore accelerates bone age and threatens to fuse the growth plates prematurely, sacrificing final adult height — a consequence that can be irreversible if the exposure continues. A left hand-and-wrist X-ray for bone age is mandatory: a bone age advanced well beyond the chronological age of 13 confirms biologically active, tissue-level estrogen excess (corroborating that the estradiol is real, not just an assay artefact) and adds urgency to removing the source. It also informs the height prognosis and the decision to use an aromatase inhibitor, which can slow further skeletal advancement once an estrogen-excess state is confirmed.
keyPoints: Estrogen drives epiphyseal fusion — high E2 advances bone age and threatens final height
Advanced bone age confirms tissue-active estrogen excess (supports a real, not artefactual, value)
Guides urgency and the role of an aromatase inhibitor

items.title: Why the treatment is cause-specific — surgery, aromatase inhibitor, or removing exposure
items.content: Therapy follows the diagnosis, not the symptom. If imaging finds an estrogen-secreting testicular or adrenal tumour, the treatment is surgical excision (with oncological staging for adrenocortical carcinoma). If genetic testing confirms aromatase excess syndrome, the mechanism-directed choice is a third-generation aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole or letrozole): blocking aromatase lowers estradiol, relieves the HPG-axis suppression so LH/FSH and endogenous testosterone can rise, allows normal male puberty, and protects against premature epiphyseal fusion and short stature. If the cause is exogenous estrogen, removing the exposure is curative. Tamoxifen, a selective estrogen-receptor modulator, is reserved for symptomatic idiopathic/pubertal gynecomastia and is the wrong tool here — it neither identifies nor removes the source and is not the agent of choice when an estrogen-overproduction state (AES) is the problem. Metabolic management of the obesity, MASLD and dyslipidaemia runs in parallel but is never the explanation for an estradiol of 296.
keyPoints: Tumour → excision; AES → third-generation aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole/letrozole); exogenous → remove it
Aromatase inhibitor (not tamoxifen) is mechanism-directed for AES — rescues the HPG axis and height
Treat obesity/MASLD in parallel, but it does not explain E2 296

items.title: How to grade the four residents' proposals
items.content: Audit each plan against the evidence. Resident 1 (start tamoxifen now): premature and unsafe — it treats an unverified number and masks a possible tumour without a diagnosis, and tamoxifen is not the agent of choice if aromatase excess is the cause. Resident 2 (diet and exercise only): necessary for the obesity, raised ALT (MASLD) and borderline LDL, but inadequate as a standalone because lifestyle cannot generate or explain an estradiol of 296 and ignores the red flag. Resident 3 ('physiological puberty, observe, plan surgery later, tamoxifen hardly helps'): the most dangerous — it misclassifies a pathological, HPG-suppressing estradiol as normal pubertal gynecomastia (which essentially never reaches ~300 pg/mL with castrate testosterone) and defers a potentially curable diagnosis. Resident 4 (send an LC-MS/MS steroid panel to explain the high estradiol): correct first instinct — verify and characterise the source. The right way forward integrates Resident 4's verification with Resident 2's parallel metabolic care, then localises and treats by cause.
keyPoints: R1 tamoxifen-now: premature/unsafe; R2 diet-only: necessary but incomplete
R3 'physiological/observe': dangerous misclassification of a pathological value
R4 LC-MS/MS steroid panel: the correct first move — verify then localise

items.title: How the way forward sequences — verify, localise, treat
items.content: Hold to a strict hierarchy. Verify: repeat estradiol and testosterone by LC-MS/MS with a full steroid panel, and take a meticulous exogenous-estrogen history. Localise (if confirmed high): scrotal ultrasound for an occult Sertoli/Leydig tumour, adrenal/abdominal CT or MRI for a feminising adrenal mass (also assessing liver and spleen), a bone-age X-ray for tissue-level estrogen activity and height risk, full LFTs with synthetic function and an abdominal Doppler ultrasound for the splenomegaly, and CYP19A1 genetic testing if an aromatase-excess pattern emerges. Treat by cause: excise a tumour, give an aromatase inhibitor for confirmed AES, remove any exogenous source, and manage obesity/MASLD/dyslipidaemia in parallel. Consider a karyotype if other features suggest it, though the low gonadotropins make hypergonadotropic Klinefelter unlikely here. The teaching point: an extreme biochemical mismatch demands assay verification and structural exclusion before symptomatic therapy or a 'physiological' label.
keyPoints: Verify (LC-MS/MS panel + exposure history) → Localise (scrotal US, adrenal/abdominal imaging, bone age, liver/spleen, CYP19A1) → Treat by cause
Low gonadotropins make hypergonadotropic Klinefelter unlikely, but karyotype if features suggest
Verify and exclude structure before treating symptoms or calling it physiological

items.stem: A 13-year-old boy has gynecomastia, estradiol 296 pg/mL, total testosterone 0.11 ng/mL, LH 0.61 and FSH 1.92 mIU/mL, and 4 mL testes. What does this hormonal pattern most suggest?
options: An autonomous estrogen source suppressing the HPG axis
Normal physiological pubertal gynecomastia
Primary testicular failure with high gonadotropins
Simple obesity-related aromatisation fully explaining the estradiol
items.explanation: An adult-range estradiol with castrate-low testosterone and suppressed LH/FSH means estrogen is being produced independently of testosterone-driven aromatisation and is feeding back to switch off gonadotropins — an autonomous estrogen source. It is not physiological puberty (estradiol never reaches ~300 with low testosterone), not primary failure (gonadotropins are low, not high), and obesity alone cannot generate this level with so little androgen substrate.

items.stem: What is the single most appropriate first step before initiating treatment for this boy's hyperestrogenism?
options: Repeat estradiol and testosterone by LC-MS/MS
Start tamoxifen empirically
Start an aromatase inhibitor empirically
Reassure and review in 6 months
items.explanation: Automated estradiol immunoassays are unreliable in boys and children, so the value must be verified by LC-MS/MS (ideally with a full steroid profile) before acting. Starting tamoxifen or an aromatase inhibitor empirically treats an unverified number and may mask pathology; reassurance ignores a major red flag.

items.stem: The boy's testes are 4 mL bilaterally with no palpable mass. What does this mean for the search for an estrogen-secreting testicular tumour?
options: A high-resolution scrotal ultrasound is still mandatory, as Sertoli/Leydig tumours can be small and non-palpable
A testicular tumour is excluded by the normal volume and examination
No imaging is needed because beta-hCG is normal
Orchidectomy should be performed empirically
items.explanation: Estrogen-secreting Sertoli and Leydig cell tumours can be sub-centimetre and non-palpable yet hormonally active, so normal volume and a normal exam do not exclude them — a high-resolution scrotal ultrasound is mandatory. A normal beta-hCG addresses hCG-secreting tumours, not estrogen-secreting gonadal stromal tumours, and empirical orchidectomy is inappropriate before localisation.

items.stem: If the steroid panel shows estrogens elevated from androgen precursors with no tumour found, and there is a family history of male gynecomastia, which diagnosis and treatment fit best?
options: Aromatase excess syndrome (CYP19A1) — treat with a third-generation aromatase inhibitor
Aromatase excess syndrome — treat with tamoxifen as first choice
Klinefelter syndrome — treat with testosterone
Physiological gynecomastia — observe only
items.explanation: Over-aromatisation of androgens to estrogens with a dominant family history is aromatase excess syndrome (gain-of-function CYP19A1), confirmed genetically. The mechanism-directed treatment is a third-generation aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole/letrozole), which lowers estradiol, relieves HPG suppression and protects height — not tamoxifen, testosterone, or observation.

items.stem: Why is a bone-age X-ray particularly important in this boy?
options: Estrogen drives epiphyseal fusion, so sustained high estradiol can advance bone age and compromise final height
It is the best test to detect a testicular tumour
It confirms the diagnosis of obesity
It measures gonadotropin levels indirectly
items.explanation: Estrogen is the main driver of skeletal maturation and epiphyseal fusion, so persistent hyperestrogenism advances bone age and threatens final adult height; an advanced bone age also corroborates that the estrogen excess is biologically active. It does not detect tumours, diagnose obesity, or measure gonadotropins.

items.stem: How should the reported splenomegaly (7 cm) and ALT 51 be handled in this work-up?
options: Investigate them (synthetic LFTs and abdominal Doppler ultrasound) — chronic liver disease causes hyperestrogenism and splenomegaly suggests structural pathology
Ignore them as incidental findings of obesity
Attribute them entirely to the high estradiol
Defer all imaging until puberty completes
items.explanation: Chronic liver disease causes hyperestrogenism and gynecomastia, and splenomegaly raises the possibility of portal hypertension or an infiltrative/storage disorder, so these findings warrant synthetic liver function tests and an abdominal Doppler ultrasound. They should not be dismissed as obesity-related or simply blamed on the estradiol.

items.stem: What does the normal beta-hCG (0.4) contribute to the differential?
options: It makes an hCG-secreting tumour (e.g. germ-cell tumour) an unlikely driver of the gynecomastia
It confirms aromatase excess syndrome
It excludes an adrenal tumour
It proves the estradiol value is accurate
items.explanation: hCG-secreting tumours drive Leydig-cell testosterone and estradiol production and cause gynecomastia, but with a raised beta-hCG; a normal beta-hCG therefore makes that mechanism unlikely. It does not confirm aromatase excess, exclude an adrenal tumour, or validate the estradiol assay.

items.stem: Which resident's proposal represents the correct first move?
options: Resident 4 — send an LC-MS/MS steroid panel to verify and characterise the high estradiol
Resident 1 — start tamoxifen with follow-up because estradiol is high
Resident 3 — call it physiological puberty, observe, and plan surgery later
Resident 2 — manage with diet and exercise alone
items.explanation: Resident 4 is correct: the extreme estradiol must be verified and characterised by LC-MS/MS before treatment. Tamoxifen-now treats an unverified number and may mask pathology; the 'physiological/observe' plan dangerously misclassifies a pathological value; and diet/exercise alone, while necessary for the obesity, cannot explain or address the hyperestrogenism.

items.statement: Physiological pubertal gynecomastia can routinely produce an estradiol of nearly 300 pg/mL with a castrate-low testosterone.
items.explanation: False. Physiological pubertal gynecomastia reflects a transient shift in the estrogen-to-androgen ratio with estradiol typically only modestly elevated; an estradiol near 300 pg/mL alongside castrate-low testosterone and suppressed gonadotropins is pathological and indicates an autonomous estrogen source.

items.statement: Automated estradiol immunoassays are reliable in prepubertal and pubertal boys and need not be confirmed by LC-MS/MS.
items.explanation: False. Immunoassays are optimised for adult-female ranges and are unreliable in males and children because of cross-reactivity, heterophile-antibody interference and poor accuracy at unusual concentrations. LC-MS/MS is the gold standard and should confirm an unexpected estradiol before action.

items.statement: If aromatase excess syndrome is confirmed, a third-generation aromatase inhibitor is preferred over tamoxifen.
items.explanation: True. In aromatase excess syndrome the problem is over-conversion of androgens to estrogens, so an aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole/letrozole) is mechanism-directed — it lowers estradiol, relieves HPG-axis suppression and protects final height. Tamoxifen, a SERM, is reserved for symptomatic idiopathic/pubertal gynecomastia.

items.assertion: The low LH and FSH in this boy are consistent with an autonomous estrogen source rather than a defect of the pituitary itself.
items.reason: Estrogen exerts potent negative feedback on the hypothalamic–pituitary axis, suppressing gonadotropin secretion.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because estrogen strongly suppresses gonadotropin release, an autonomous estrogen source will lower LH and FSH without any intrinsic pituitary lesion — exactly the pattern seen here. Hence option 0.

items.assertion: Tamoxifen should be started immediately as the definitive treatment for this boy's hyperestrogenism.
items.reason: Tamoxifen reliably identifies and removes the underlying source of excess estrogen.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Tamoxifen is a symptomatic SERM reserved for idiopathic/pubertal gynecomastia and is not the definitive treatment for an undiagnosed hyperestrogenic state; it neither identifies nor removes the source, and it is not the agent of choice if aromatase excess is the cause. Hence option 4.

items.assertion: The splenomegaly and raised ALT in this boy warrant their own structural work-up.
items.reason: Chronic liver disease can cause hyperestrogenism and gynecomastia, and splenomegaly may indicate portal hypertension or an infiltrative disorder.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because hepatic disease contributes to hyperestrogenism/gynecomastia and unexplained splenomegaly can signal portal hypertension or an infiltrative/storage disorder, these findings justify synthetic liver function tests and abdominal Doppler imaging rather than being dismissed. Hence option 0.
